did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used AI/ML methods for automated diagnosis of Lentigo on Reflectance Confocal Microscopy (RCM) images. Specifically, it implemented three feature extraction methods: Wavelets, Haralick features, and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) via Transfer Learning using the InceptionV3 architecture. These features were fed into linear Support Vector Machines (SVM) for classification tasks (Healthy, Benign, and Malignant Lentigo) using both whole-image and patch-based approaches.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the implementation of two schemes (whole-image and patch-based) for automated diagnosis of Lentigo using Reflectance Confocal Microscopy images. It details specific methodologies including feature extraction (Wavelets, Haralick, CNN via Transfer Learning with InceptionV3) and classification (SVM), describes the construction of a custom dataset (2098 RCM images from 60 patients), and reports quantitative results (F1-Scores) from experiments conducted on this data. It is published in the IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal and Image Processing.

title: Two Schemes for Automated Diagnosis of Lentigo on Confocal Microscopy Images
journal: IEEE 4th International Conference on Signal and Image Processing
brief_summary: This study investigates automated diagnosis of Healthy, Benign, and Malignant Lentigo using Reflectance Confocal Microscopy (RCM) images. The authors implemented three feature extraction methods—Wavelets, Haralick features, and Convolutional Neural Networks via Transfer Learning with InceptionV3—within two classification schemes: whole-image and patch-based approaches. The dataset consisted of 2098 RCM images from 60 patients, with a subset of 2307 thumbnails extracted for patch-level analysis. Evaluation was performed using nested cross-validation with F1-Score metrics to assess performance on unbalanced classes. Results indicated that Transfer Learning with Average pooling achieved the best classification performance across both schemes. The study concludes that deep learning features combined with patch-based merging provide robust diagnostic capabilities for RCM images.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as absence of Malignant and Benign characteristics. Used in 3-class classification (Healthy vs Benign vs Malignant) and binary classification (Healthy vs Lesional). Dataset contained 259 healthy thumbnails.

Classified as images composed of benign pathology. Used in 3-class classification (Healthy vs Benign vs Malignant) and binary classification (Non Malignant vs Malignant). Dataset contained 1622 benign thumbnails.

Classified as images including at least malignant information. Used in 3-class classification (Healthy vs Benign vs Malignant) and binary classification (Non Malignant vs Malignant). Dataset contained 426 malignant thumbnails.
Used in binary classification task 'Healthy vs Lesional' (H vs L). Represents tissue containing either benign or malignant characteristics.
Used in binary classification task 'Non Malignant vs Malignant' (NM vs M). Composed of Healthy and Benign classes.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic categories: Healthy, Benign, and Malignant Lentigo; also binary tasks: Non Malignant vs Malignant, and Healthy vs Lesional tissue.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly investigates classification on RCM images into three categories (Healthy, Benign, Malignant) and binary categories (Non Malignant vs Malignant, Healthy vs Lesional). The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels using linear SVMs fed by features from Wavelets, Haralick, or CNNs. Performance is measured using F1-Scores for these discrete classes.

datasets_used.name: Saint-Etienne Hospital RCM Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private dataset provided by the dermatology service of Saint-Etienne hospital under patient consent. It contains Clinical Photography, Dermatoscopy, and Reflectance Confocal Microscopy (RCM) images. The study focuses on the RCM images related to Lentigo pathology, acquired from the dermoepidermal junction. The dataset includes ground truth labels from histopathology.

use.training: Used as the primary source for the 'Original images' dataset (2098 annotated RCM images) for training and testing via nested cross-validation.
use.validation: Used for validation via nested cross-validation (5-fold) to evaluate prediction models and hyperparameters.
use.testing: Used for testing via nested cross-validation to assess classification performance (F1-Score).
use.other: A subset of 2307 thumbnails (259 healthy, 1622 benign, 426 malignant) was extracted from this dataset for patch-level analysis.

datasets_used.name: ImageNet
datasets_used.description: A large-scale hierarchical image database used as the pre-training source for the InceptionV3 architecture.

use.training: Used for pre-training the InceptionV3 Convolutional Neural Network before applying Transfer Learning to the RCM dataset.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To cross-validate hyperparameters and evaluate prediction models objectively, reducing bias compared to simple cross-validation.
To perform the cross-validation steps within the nested cross-validation framework.
To evaluate classification performance, chosen for its suitability for unbalanced populations as it provides information about recall and precision.
To analyze the stability of models along the nested cross-validation process.
To normalize extracted features by subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation to make the classification task more accurate and robust.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Performance was evaluated using F1-Scores and standard deviations derived from nested cross-validation.

Saint-Etienne Hospital RCM Dataset (Orig.description: 2098 annotated RCM images from 60 patients. Evaluated using nested cross-validation (5-fold) with F1-Score.
Saint-Etienne Hospital RCM Dataset (Orig.notes: Transfer Learning methods significantly outperformed handcrafted methods. Average Pooling performed better than Max Pooling for whole-image classification.

Saint-Etienne Hospital RCM Dataset (Thum.description: Subset of 2307 thumbnails (259 healthy, 1622 benign, 426 malignant) extracted from original images. Evaluated using nested cross-validation (5-fold) with F1-Score.
Saint-Etienne Hospital RCM Dataset (Thum.notes: Results for Max and Avg pooling were similar for thumbnails. Transfer Learning significantly outperformed handcrafted methods.

Saint-Etienne Hospital RCM Dataset (Slid.description: Patch-based classification using sliding window on original images. Merged via Decision level or Score level. Evaluated using nested cross-validation (5-fold) with F1-Score. Tasks: 3-class (H vs B vs M), Healthy vs Lesional (H vs L), Non Malignant vs Malignant (NM vs M).
Saint-Etienne Hospital RCM Dataset (Slid.notes: Score level merging was more efficient than Decision level. Overlap slightly improved results. Classification of NM vs M was more significant than H vs L.

architecture_details.modification: Last layers devoted to classification were removed; Transfer Learning approach used instead of training from scratch

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly reported (linear SVM used for final classification)
training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly reported (linear SVM used for final classification)
training_details.hyperparameters: Features reduced using Generalized Gaussian Distribution (keeping alpha and beta coefficients) for Wavelets; Standard score normalization (Z-score) applied to all features

input_features: Wavelets (Daubechies wavelets along horizontal, vertical, diagonal axes)
Haralick features (statistical descriptors along horizontal, vertical, two diagonals)
CNN features (from InceptionV3 with Max/Avg pooling)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Wavelets (Daubechies wavelets with Generalized Gaussian Distribution reduction)
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) via Transfer Learning using InceptionV3 architecture
Linear Support Vector Machine (SVM) with simple linear kernel
preprocessing_techniques: Standard score normalization (Z-score) applied to extracted features by subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation to make the classification task more accurate and robust.
Patch extraction (thumbnails) of 250x250 pixels from original 1000x1000 pixel RCM images.
Sliding window approach for patch-based classification.
Feature reduction using Generalized Gaussian Distribution (keeping alpha and beta coefficients) for Wavelets.
Removal of last classification layers from InceptionV3 for Transfer Learning.
conclusions: Transfer Learning methods significantly outperform handcrafted feature extraction methods (Wavelets and Haralick) for the automated diagnosis of Lentigo on RCM images, achieving the highest F1-Scores and lowest standard deviations.

For whole-image classification, Transfer Learning with Global Average Pooling yields better performance than Global Maximum Pooling, likely because it preserves more spatial information in the activation map.
For patch-level (thumbnail) classification, Transfer Learning with Global Maximum Pooling and Global Average Pooling yields similar performance.
In the patch-based scheme, merging prediction scores (Score level) is more efficient than merging final decisions (Decision level) for improving classification accuracy.
Introducing an overlap between patches in the sliding window approach slightly improves classification results compared to non-overlapping patches.

The classification task distinguishing Non Malignant from Malignant tissues is more significant (higher performance) than distinguishing Healthy from Lesional tissue, which may be due to the visual similarity between Healthy and Benign tissues.

The study concludes that deep learning features combined with patch-based merging provide robust diagnostic capabilities for RCM images, though future work will investigate fine-tuning the last layers of the CNN and novel methods for merging decisions.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single-center, private dataset from Saint-Etienne Hospital (2098 images from 60 patients), limiting generalizability and raising concerns about external validation and population bias.

The dataset is unbalanced, particularly in the thumbnail subset (1622 benign vs. 259 healthy vs. 426 malignant), which necessitates the use of F1-Score but may skew model performance towards the majority class.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; significance was inferred from F1-Score magnitudes and standard deviations from nested cross-validation.
The patch-based scheme uses a sliding window with a 50% overlap, which may introduce data leakage or redundancy between adjacent patches, potentially inflating performance metrics.
Transfer Learning was used with ImageNet pre-training; while effective, this approach assumes feature compatibility between natural images and RCM medical images, which may not hold universally.
The paper does not report results for fine-tuning the last layers of the CNN, noting it as future work, which limits the assessment of the full potential of the deep learning approach.

Ground truth labels were provided by specialists and histopathology, but the study acknowledges that images may contain mixed tissue types (malignant, benign, healthy), complicating binary classification tasks like 'Healthy vs. Lesional'.
The study focuses only on Lentigo pathology, limiting the clinical relevance to this specific condition and not addressing broader dermatological applications.
